design_philosophy.core_loop: Travel to historical time periods, gather contextual information about artifacts and historical figures, answer fill-in-the-blank questions to return artifacts to their proper era
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Educational knowledge verification vs. platforming exploration; risk of being sent back to present if answers are incorrect (max 2 wrong attempts per artifact)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Time-traveling detective solving historical mysteries and preserving timeline integrity by reuniting artifacts with their origins
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Prioritizes historical education and knowledge-testing over challenge; NES version removes damage/death mechanics entirely, making progression about comprehension rather than execution; treats Mario as an educational tool rather than action hero

difficulty.lives_system: No lives system; player cannot die or take damage in NES version
difficulty.continues_system: Password system allows resuming from specific game states
difficulty.checkpointing: Hub-based checkpointing via Bowser's museum; password system between sessions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls pacing of exploration and learning; two incorrect answers triggers restart of current artifact task; no fail state or game over possible

progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear unlock of time periods and artifacts; player must return artifacts sequentially to progress; final boss encounter requires completion of all artifact returns plus three multiple-choice questions
replayability_hooks: Multiple time periods to explore (14 selectable in NES version)
Different historical figures and artifacts across periods
Three random final quiz questions create variation in end-game sequence

world_building_techniques: NPC dialogue providing historical context and facts about time period inhabitants
Message blocks offering hints about artifact origins
Historical figure depictions as NPCs in their native time periods
Artifact-to-person associations (e.g., Apple → Isaac Newton, 1687 Cambridge)
Introductory documents with missing terms that establish historical significance
Multiple ending cutscenes based on player performance (success variations: Bowser trapped in Jurassic era vs. escape to Paradise)

boss_design.philosophy: Final confrontation contingent on accumulated knowledge; Bowser serves as narrative catalyst rather than skill-based obstacle; defeat tied to quiz completion rather than combat execution
notable_bosses: Bowser - Final encounter after completing all artifact returns and answering three historical multiple-choice questions; fight mechanics not detailed in sources

faction_indicators: Time period allegiances (learning alignment with specific historical eras)
danger_signals: Repeated failed answers indicate knowledge gaps in specific historical periods
Inability to locate artifacts suggests exploration avoidance
environmental_storytelling: Bowser's museum displays stolen artifacts, establishing theft as central plot
Time period environments visually represent historical contexts
NPC presence grounds artifact significance in human history
Timulator mechanics (surfing through ocean to collect mushrooms for temporal transport) establish time travel as resource-dependent
